segments.s708-g49: به خسرو چنین گفت مریم که من is a fronted indirect speech clause: به + خسرو marks the addressee ('to Khosrow'), چنین 'thus' comments on the manner, گفت is the 3rd-person past of گفتن ('said'), and مریم is the speaker. که introduces the content clause; من begins that subordinate clause as the subject ('I'). Word order is typical classical Persian: indirect speech marker (به ... گفت) + speaker + که + subject.
segments.s708-g50: بپا آورم جنگ این انجمن is the content of what Maryam says. بپا آوردن + noun is a verbal phrase meaning 'to set up/raise' (here in first person). آورم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive form attached to بپا to express intention ('I will/shall raise'). جنگ این انجمن is the direct object (جنگ 'war' + این انجمن 'this assembly'), with typical noun phrase order adjective/determiner after noun in poetic usage.
segments.s708-g51: به من ده سرافراز بندوی را is an imperative/request structure: (ده) is the omitted imperative 'give' understood from context, به من marks the recipient ('to me' / 'give me'), سرافراز بندوی is the object noun phrase ('proud/honoured bondmen'), and را is the definite-object marker emphasizing the direct object. Word order (recipient before verb/imperative) is common in this epic style.
segments.s708-g52: که تا رومیان از پی روی را begins a purpose clause introduced by که تا ('so that' / 'in order that'). تا here signals purpose rather than temporal 'until'. رومیان is the subject of the following verb (implied in the next line), from marks relation ('from' / 'out of') and پی ('after; in pursuit of') links to روی ('face'); را is the object marker attached to what the Romans are to see in the continuation. Grammatically this fragment prepares a subordinate clause expressing the goal of giving the bondmen.
segments.s708-g53: ببینند و باز آرمش تن درست: ببینند is the 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive ('they may see' / 'they should see'), used after تا for purpose. و links clauses. باز 'again' modifies the following phrase. آرمش تن درست is a noun phrase meaning 'the body's proper rest' (آرمش 'rest', تن 'body', درست 'right/proper') with the adjective درست placed after the noun in this poetic order.
segments.s708-g54: کسی بیهوده جنگ هرگز نجست is an independent remark: کسی here, combined with هرگز ... نجست, functions negatively ('no one ever'). بیهوده ('in vain') qualifies جنگ ('war'), and نجست is the past tense of جستن used in the sense 'to seek'—so the clause means 'no one ever sought war in vain.' The negative sense arises from the combination of کسی + هرگز + negative meaning of the verb form.
segments.s708-g55: فرستاد بندوی را شهریار is a simple past transitive clause: فرستاد is the 3rd-person singular past of فرستادن ('sent'), بندوی را is the definite direct object with را marking (‘the bondman’), and شهریار is the agent/subject 'the king' placed after the object as is common in poetic narrative order.
segments.s708-g56: به نزد نیاطوس با ده سوار gives the destination and accompaniment: به نزد literally 'to the presence of' (to/at the court of) + نیاطوس the proper name, and با ده سوار 'with ten riders/horsemen' where با is the preposition 'with' and سوار ('rider') is bound to the numeral 'ten' (implied in the printed line). This phrase describes to whom he was sent and with what escort.
segments.s708-g73: مده is a negative imperative (poetic variant) meaning 'do not (do)'; رنج و کردار is a coordinated noun phrase 'suffering and conduct/deeds' with و joining the two nouns; قیصر is the possessed/affected noun 'Caesar'; بباد is a short verblike predicate meaning 'be lost / go to waste' (impersonal/passive sense). The clause literally says 'Do not — Caesar's suffering and deeds — be lost.'
segments.s708-g74: بمان is an imperative 'remain, stay'; تا introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that'); به before باشیم is the classical marker used with the subjunctive, so به باشیم = 'that we may be'; چند شاد is an adverbial/temporal phrase 'a little / for a short while merry'. Together: 'Stay so that we may be a little merry.'
segments.s708-g75: بکین is an imperative (poetic form of بکن) 'do (it)' or 'act'; پدر من addresses 'my father'; جگر خسته‌ام is a predicative phrase 'my jigar (heart/seat of feeling) is weary' with خسته‌ام = 'I am weary' attached to the noun. The speaker commands/urges and immediately reports personal exhaustion.
segments.s708-g76: کمر بر میان is a spatial phrase 'around the middle at the waist' (کمر = waist / girdle, میان = middle); سوک را is the definite object 'the mourning (belt/rite)' with را marking it; بسته‌ام is the perfective 'I have tied/bound.' The whole segment: 'I have bound the mourning (belt) about my waist.'
segments.s708-g77: دل او is 'his heart'; سراسر modifies the following predicate as 'entirely / throughout'; پر از + noun is the adjectival construction 'full of ...' so پر از کین = 'full of hatred'; اوست is the copula 'he/it is'. Word order is typical Persian: topic (دل او) + adverb (سراسر) + predicate (پر از کین) + copula.
segments.s708-g78: زبانش = 'his tongue / his speech' (ش suffix = his); پر از رنج = 'full of suffering/complaint'; و links the next element; تیماراوست is compressed: تیمار + اوست, meaning 'his care/concern is (so)' or 'he is full of care' — here it functions as a parallel predicate to the previous clause. So: 'his tongue is full of suffering and his care likewise.'
segments.s708-g79: که introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause 'for / that'; او از پی = 'he, in following / after' (از پی = after/in pursuit); واژ شد is a classical/perf. passive-like cluster 'was overturned / turned over / overturned in judgment'; زشت گوی is a compound nominal phrase 'one who speaks badly / an ill-speaker' (زشت modifying گوی). The clause explains that he, having been overturned, became someone who utters ugly words.
segments.s708-g80: تو = 'you' (address); از introduces the source ('from/out of'); بی‌خرد = 'the senseless / foolish person'; هوشمندی is the abstract 'wisdom/intelligence'; مجوی is an imperative negative 'do not seek.' The clause admonishes: 'Do not seek wisdom from a fool.'
segments.s708-g25: نیاطوس is a proper name (subject). زان is an archaic/poetic preposition meaning “from that” and introduces the place of departure. جایگه (‘place’) is the object of زان, and برنشست (a past action) describes his movement from that place; here بر-نِشست is used in the simple past sense of taking a position/mounting or getting up from the spot.
segments.s708-g26: به لشکرگه خویش is a prepositional phrase: به (towards/into) + لشکرگه (camp/army-encampment) + خویش split into خوی + ش, where خوی supplies the lexical base and ش is the enclitic possessive ‘his/own’. شد is the past copular/become verb here meaning “became,” followed by نیم مست, an adjectival phrase ‘half-drunk’ (نیم = half, مست = drunk).
segments.s708-g27: بپوشید is a past-tense verb with the prefix بـ- and here means ‘put on’ or ‘doned’. رومی is the agent (the Roman), and زره رزم را is the object: زره (armor) modified by رزم (‘of battle’); را is the definite/object marker marking زره رزم as the direct object.
segments.s708-g28: ز بهر is an older/poetic periphrastic preposition meaning ‘for the sake of’ or ‘in order to’. تبه کردن is a verbal phrase where تبه (ruin/mischief) plus کردن (light verb ‘to do/cause’) yields ‘to ruin’ or ‘to cause mischief’. بزم را (bāzm + object marker را) names the goal/target of that action: the feast/assembly to be ruined.
segments.s708-g29: سواران رومی is the noun phrase ‘Roman horsemen’ (سواران = riders/horsemen, رومی = Roman). همه intensifies it as ‘all’ or ‘every one’. جنگ جوی is a compound: جنگ (war/battle) + جوی (seeking/searching), forming the adjective/noun ‘war-seeking’ or ‘eager for battle’ that modifies the horsemen.
segments.s708-g30: به درگاه خسرو is a prepositional phrase: به (toward/to) + درگاه (the royal court/presence) + خسرو (Khosrow, proper name) as the locus. نهادند is the past-tense plural verb ‘they placed/set/put’ (here used idiomatically), and روی (face) is its object; نهادند روی together means ‘they directed/turned their faces’ toward the court.
segments.s708-g31: هم آنگه is a temporal adverbial ‘just then/at that moment’ (هم reinforcing آنگه). ز لشکر marks origin ‘from the host/army’ (ز = from). سواری here denotes the mounted force/cavalry; چو باد is a simile ‘like the wind’ modifying the speed/character of that cavalry.
segments.s708-g32: به خسرو is the directional phrase ‘to Khosrow’ (به = to/toward). فرستاد is the past tense verb ‘sent’ (third person singular or plural depending on the subject), and رومی نژاد at the end is the agent phrase ‘of Roman stock’ / ‘the Romans’ (نژاد = race/lineage), indicating who dispatched the force.
segments.s708-g65: تو is the emphatic 2nd-person pronoun placed before the object (typical SOV order). پیوند خویشی is a compound noun phrase 'the bond/tie of kinship' acting as the direct object. همی‌برکنی is a classical/poetic present form with the prefix همی- marking continuous/habitual action and the 2nd-person singular ending -ی: literally 'you keep breaking' or 'you are breaking (repeatedly)'.
segments.s708-g66: همان is a demonstrative/adverbial intensifier 'that very/just the same'. فر here denotes the royal/divine glory associated with kings. قیصر is the source/possessor name 'Caesar' used appositively to qualify فر. ز + من marks the origin/target 'from me' (ز = 'from'); بفگنی is an archaic 2nd-person singular verbal form (prefix ب- + verb stem) used here to indicate subject's action toward the speaker — roughly 'you take/strip (away)'. The clause thus means 'you will strip that Caesar-glory from me.'
segments.s708-g67: ز قیصر sets the source 'from Caesar' (poetic preposition ز). شنیدی is 2nd-person past 'you heard' but here functions idiomatically as 'Have you heard (that)...' and is followed by the subordinating که introducing the reported content. خسرو is the subject of the following subordinate clause. ز دین means 'from the religion/faith' (ز = 'from', دین = 'religion').
segments.s708-g68: بگردد is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive form of گشتن/گردیدن used to mean 'turn (away)' or 'become' here with intransitive sense 'turn (away)'. چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آید is 3rd-person present/subjunctive 'he comes'. به ایران‌زمین is the goal phrase 'to the land of Iran'. Together: 'that Khosrow will turn from the faith when he comes to Iran.'
segments.s708-g69: مگو is the negative imperative 'do not say/utter'. ایچ is an emphatic indefinite 'anything/any' (here 'any/none' in negative context: 'do not say any...'). گفتار is the noun 'speech/words'. نا before دلپذیر negates the adjective, so نا دلپذیر = 'unpleasant'. The phrase as a whole: 'Do not utter any unpleasant words.'
segments.s708-g70: تو is the vocative/emphatic 'you' placed before the command. بندوی (بندوی) is the direct object 'the bondman/servant' and را is the definite object marker. سر به آغوش گیر is an idiomatic verbal phrase: سر 'head' + به آغوش 'to (your) bosom/embrace' + گیر (imperative/stem 'take, seize') = 'take him into your arms / lay his head on your breast'. The verb is imperative-directed to 'تو'.
segments.s708-g71: ندانی is 2nd-person present negative 'you do not know' (classical style). که introduces a subordinate clause 'that ...'. دهقان here refers to the landed gentry/dihqān (social class) as subject. ز دین کهن = 'from the ancient religion' (ز = 'from', دین = 'religion', کهن = 'old/ancient'), setting what the dehqān does not abandon.
segments.s708-g72: نپیچد is the 3rd-person negative present of پیچیدن/پیچیدن meaning 'does not turn/deviate' (here 'does not abandon/turn away'). چرا is the interrogative 'why' (here rhetorical: 'why then...'). خام is an adjective 'naive/unripe' modifying the subsequent speech. گویی in the verse context functions as a verbal 'you say' (present 2nd-person), but the provided lexical senses are nominal ('conversation, dialogue') and do not match the verbal use — so no listed sense fits. سخن is the noun 'words/speech'. The clause therefore asks 'why do you then speak naive words?'
segments.s708-g41: A string of names giving a range: کیومرث and جمشید are names joined by و (and). تا + کی قباد marks the endpoint ‘up to Kay Qobad’ (تا = “until/up to”); کی here is the title element (Kay) attached to قباد, forming the name 'Kay Qobad' rather than the interrogative 'who'. Word order lists the start and end of the range.
segments.s708-g42: A negative statement with an impersonal subject: کسی ... نکردند یاد literally ‘no one ... did (make) remembrance’. از marks the relationship ‘concerning/of the Messiah’ and نکردند is the 3rd-person plural past negative of کردن used with یاد to form ‘remember/mention’. The clause order is somewhat literary (object before verb).
segments.s708-g43: مبادا که is an optative/negative introducer meaning ‘may it not be / lest that’; که introduces the subordinate clause. دین نیاکان خویش is a possessive noun phrase: دین (religion) modified by نیاکان خویش ‘of his ancestors’ (خویش here realized as خوی + ش in the data: kinship/own + possessive suffix).
segments.s708-g44: گزیده is an adjective/noun ‘chosen, select’ modifying the following coordinated nouns سرافراز و پاکان (‘the proud/lofty and the pure’). خویش (here shown as خوی + ش) again is the possessive ‘his own’, so the phrase means ‘his chosen, the proud and his pure ones’. The construction is elliptical (a copula or verb is omitted but implied).
segments.s708-g45: گذارد/گذارم here functions as a jussive/wish ‘may it be granted/let (it be) that I...’. ب = ‘into / to’ and دین مسیحا = ‘the religion of the Messiah’ (i.e., becoming a Christian); شوم is 1st-person singular subjunctive ‘may I become’. Together they express a wish to be allowed to become one of the Messiah’s followers.
segments.s708-g46: This is a parallel wish/negation: نگیرم is negative subjunctive/optative ‘may I not be taken/seized’ (or ‘I will not accept being…’), بخوان + واژ functions as ‘be called/named “waž/overturned”’ (بخوان here = ‘to call/name’ in a literary sense), و links the clause, and ترسا شوم is again 1st-person subjunctive ‘may I become a teresā (a pious/saintly person)’. The clause states refusal to be seized and labeled and to become a sanctimonious/pious person.
segments.s708-g47: A present-tense statement: تو (you) + تنها همی (you alone, still/always) sets the topic. کژگیری is the object meaning ‘fault‑finding / crookedness’, and شمار is the verb ‘you count/you reckon’ (here functioning as 2nd-person present). Word order is subject – adverbials – object – verb, typical of poetic diction.
segments.s708-g48: هنر (skill, art) is the object of دیدم (‘I saw’), a simple past 1st-person. از رومیان means ‘from/among the Romans’ (i.e., I observed it in the Romans). روز کار is a temporal phrase ‘the day/time of action’ (literally ‘day of work’) meaning ‘in times of activity’ or ‘on the field of action’.
segments.s708-g33: This clause begins with the relative/exclamatory particle 'که' introducing the offending person ('who'/'that'). 'بندوی' is a noun meaning a bondman/servant and is qualified by the adjective 'ناکس' (wretched/vile). 'چرا' is an interrogative 'why' asking for the reason or daring of the action, and 'پشت دست' is a compound meaning 'the back of the hand' (پشت = back, دست = hand). Word order is typical classical Persian: relative particle + noun phrase + interrogative.
segments.s708-g34: 'زند' here is an older/poetic present-tense form of زدن (3rd person singular) meaning 'he strikes' or 'he slaps' (the provided sense list does not include this verbal sense, so it is represented as the archaic/poetic verb). 'بر' is the preposition 'on/upon', and 'رخ مرد یزدان‌پرست' is the noun phrase 'the face of a God‑worshipping man' (رخ = face, مرد = man, یزدان‌پرست = pious/God‑worshipper). The verb governs the prepositional phrase indicating the target of the blow.
segments.s708-g35: 'گر' is the conditional 'if'. 'او را' is the definite object marker construction ('him' as direct object). 'فرستی' is the 2nd person present/subjunctive or imperative form of فرستادن meaning 'you send' or 'send (him)'. 'به نزدیک من' is a prepositional phrase 'to my presence' or 'near me' (به = to, نزدیک = near, من = me as object). The clause is a conditional instruction directed at the addressee.
segments.s708-g36: The coordinating 'و' links an alternative; 'گرنه' means 'otherwise / if not'. 'ببین' is the 2nd person imperative of دیدن ('see' or 'observe'). 'شورش انجمن' is a noun phrase meaning 'the tumult/revolt of the assembly' (شورش = tumult/rebellion; انجمن = assembly). The whole phrase functions as a warning: if you do not send him, witness the assembly's uproar.
segments.s708-g37: 'ز' (poetic form of از) is the preposition 'from'. 'من' is the object 'me'. 'بیش' is an adverb meaning 'more' or 'farther', and 'پیچی' is the 2nd person present/subjunctive or imperative of پیچیدن used here as an instruction 'turn (away)'. 'کنون' means 'now'. The short clause is an imperative: 'turn farther away from me now.' 'کز رهی' begins the following relative clause (کز = 'who/that/of which', رهی = wayfarer/person of the road).
segments.s708-g38: 'که' introduces a relative clause 'who'. 'جوید' is the present-tense verb 'seeks' (archaic form of جستن/جویدن), and 'همی' is a particle marking habitual/continuous action ('continually'/'always'). 'تخت شاهنشهی' is a compound/genitival phrase 'the throne of kingship / the imperial throne' (تخت = throne; شاهنشهی = kingship/imperial rule). Together: 'who continually seeks the imperial throne.'
segments.s708-g39: 'چو' is the temporal particle 'when'. 'بشنید' is the 3rd person singular past 'he heard'. 'خسرو' is the subject 'Khosrow'. 'برآشفت' is a past-tense verbal compound meaning 'he became angry/was enraged'. The coordinating 'و گفت' ('and said') introduces reported speech. Word order is standard narrative: temporal particle + verb + subject + result/action + speech marker.
segments.s708-g40: The particle 'که' introduces the content of Khosrow's speech. 'کس' here functions in a negative/hard prohibition sense ('let no one' / 'no one'), 'دین یزدان' is the object 'the religion/faith of God' (دین = religion, یزدان = God). The two final items 'نیارد نهفت' are archaic/poetic verbal forms conveying prohibition against concealing or keeping the faith hidden; in sense-listing they do not match regular modern lemmas, so they represent an older negative potential/imperative idea: 'let no one hide/conceal' (i.e., 'do not hide the religion of God'). Grammatically this is a prohibitive content clause introduced by گفت.
segments.s708-g17: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause ('when'). بندوی is the agent ('the bondman/servant') and دید is a past-tense verb ('saw'); the remainder (آن بزد پشت دست) is a reported action: آن is 'that one' (subject of the following verb), بزد is past 3sg 'struck', and پشت دست is the object phrase 'the back of the hand'. Word order is typical older Persian (temporal marker → subject → verb → reported clause).
segments.s708-g18: بخوان is an imperative 'recite/read aloud'. It is followed by a stacked prepositional phrase (بر + به + روی) meaning roughly 'upon/to the face of' — poetic doubling of prepositions is common in classical Persian. چلیپا پرست is a compound idea 'cross‑worshipper' (چلیپا = cross, پرست = worshipper/‑worshipper). Together the phrase gives a command directed at addressing or reading something to the cross‑worshipper's face.
segments.s708-g19: غمی گشت زان کار is an impersonal/result clause 'grief arose from that deed' (گشت = 'became/arose'). خسرو چودید is a temporal comment 'Khosrow, when he saw (it)' — چودید is an archaic/poetic rendering of 'چو دید' ('when he saw'), linking the emotional result to the king's perception.
segments.s708-g20: برخساره شد uses برخساره ('ruined' / 'in loss') with شد (past 'became') to say his appearance/fortune was damaged. چون is the comparative 'like/as', گل is 'flower', and شنبلید names a plant (fenugreek) used as a simile; the whole phrase compares his withering/paleness to that flower. The sequence is verb-final descriptive after the copula شد, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s708-g21: به marks the indirect object 'to' (here 'to Gostaham' — گستهم is a proper name). گفت is the past 'said' (here reported speech), and the following گزاره این گو بی‌خرد is the quoted imperative: این گو = 'say this' (این = 'this', گو = imperative 'say'), while بی‌خرد is a vocative 'O foolish one'. Word order places the indirect object before the verb, then the quoted imperative.
segments.s708-g22: نباید is an impersonal negative ('must not' / 'one should not'). که introduces the subordinate clause giving the content/reason. بی‌داوری is an adverbial/nominal phrase 'without judgment', and می‌خورد is a present habitual/impersonal verbal form here meaning 'it happens / is done' (idiomatically 'ought not to be done'). Together: 'one ought not to do [something] without judgment.'
segments.s708-g23: ورا is a directional/indirect-object pronoun 'to/for him' (or 'of him'), followed by با نیاطوس رومی ('with Niatous the Roman'). چه کار is a fixed interrogative phrase 'what business/affair' — together: 'What business does he have with Niatous the Roman?' The phrase orders the prepositional complement before the interrogative head, idiomatic in older Persian.
segments.s708-g24: تن خویش را is the definite direct object 'his own body' (خوی + ش = خویش 'his own'), with را marking the object. کرد is the past 'made/caused', and امروز خوار is a predicative phrase 'today despised' — the whole means 'he has made himself (his body) despised today' (i.e., brought himself low). The agent is implied in context (he/that person).
segments.s708-g57: همان نیز مریم زن هوشمند is a noun phrase headed by همان (a demonstrative) followed by نیز (an additive particle) and the proper name مریم with the appositive noun phrase زن هوشمند. Word order is demonstrative + particle + name + noun + adjective (the adjective هوشمند follows the noun it modifies, as is normal in Persian poetry).
segments.s708-g58: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. بودی is a poetic/past form of بودن ('was'); here it functions as past-tense copula. همیشه is the adverb 'always'. لبانش (لبان + ـش) is the possessed noun 'her lips'. بپند is a contracted/poetic verbal form from پنداشتن ('to consider/think') used here in a stative/active sense; the clause literally links 'her lips' with that verb. Together the clause reads roughly 'who was always (with) her lips b-pend' — idiomatically rendered as 'who always kept her lips (i.e. kept silent/guarded her speech)' in this context.
segments.s708-g59: بدو is the clitic‑like prepositional phrase 'to him/unto him' (به + او compressed). گفت here is the verb 'said' (past), and رو is the imperative of رفتن ('go') in classical/poetic Persian (i.e. 'go'), followed by a prepositional phrase با برادر پدر 'to/with the father's brother' (با + برادر + پدر). Word order: indirect object (بدو) + reporting verb (گفت) + imperative (رو) + prepositional goal (با برادر پدر).
segments.s708-g60: بگو‌ای is the vocative/quotative formula 'say, O ...' (بگو + ای as addressor). بداندیش and پرخاشخر are vocative epithets applied to the addressee: بداندیش 'ill‑minded/malicious' (adjective) and پرخاشخر 'quarrelsome/aggressive one' (a noun/adjective used as a disparaging epithet). The whole segment is the quoted command the speaker tells the messenger to utter.
segments.s708-g61: ندیدی is the negative past interrogative 'did you not see' (ن + دیدی), with چرا/که omitted because this is rhetorical. که introduces the content clause explaining what was seen. با شاه قیصر is a prepositional phrase 'to/with King Caesar' (با + title + name). چه گفت = 'what he said' (چه = 'what'; گفت is the past verb 'said'). The clause order follows subject/agent-elided + verb phrase typical of Persian narrative.
segments.s708-g62: ز بهر is a classical poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'for the sake of' (ز = poetic 'from/of', بهر = 'sake/for'). بزرگی is the abstract noun 'greatness'. ورا (ve + را contracted or و + را) here means 'to him' (ورا = به + او in poetic form). بود is the past copula 'was'. جفت is a predicate noun meaning 'pair/match/mate' (here 'a match' or 'equal'). Structure: 'for the sake of greatness he was a match for him.'
segments.s708-g63: ز پیوند خویشی is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from/by the bond of kinship' (ز = poetic 'from/through', پیوند 'bond/link', خویشی 'kinship'). و is the coordinating 'and'. از خواسته is another prepositional phrase 'from/by desire/favor' (از = 'from/of', خواسته = 'wish/desire' or 'what is sought'); together these phrases list the sources or grounds of his standing.
segments.s708-g64: ز مردان وز گنج آراسته is a verbless/adjective‑predicate construction typical in Persian poetry: ز (from/by) مردان (men) و ز (and by/from) گنج (treasure) آراسته (adorned). Note وز here is a poetic contraction of و + ز (and + from), but the supplied senses do not list that contraction explicitly. آراسته is a past participle/adjective meaning 'adorned' or 'decked out', so the whole means 'adorned by men and by treasure.'
segments.s708-g1: دگر روز is a time phrase meaning ‘the next/another day’ (دگر = ‘other/another’) followed by the noun روز. خسرو is the subject (proper name). بیاراست is a past tense / perfective verbal form from برقرار ساختن / آراستن meaning ‘he adorned / he prepared himself’ and follows the subject (typical S V order). گاه at the end here works as a temporal adverb ‘at that time’ or ‘for a moment’, linking to the following clause.
segments.s708-g2: به سر برنهاد is a light‑verb construction: به + سر (on/to the head) + برنهاد (past of نهادن here meaning ‘placed on’). آن qualifies the following noun as ‘that/the’ and کیانی is an adjective/epithet ‘Kayanian / royal’ modifying کلاه ‘hat/crown’. Word order is prepositional phrase (به سر) + verb + demonstrative + adjective + noun.
segments.s708-g3: نهادند is a past plural form of نهادن (‘they set/placed’). در گلشن is a prepositional phrase ‘in the garden’ (گلشن = garden). سور خوان combines two nouns for the feast/banquet setting (سور = feast, خوان = table/banquet) and together indicate that a banquet was arranged there. The clause is straightforward S (implicit agents) + V + locative complement.
segments.s708-g4: چنین گفت is a formulaic expression ‘thus he said’ (چنین = thus). پس here has the temporal/sequence meaning ‘then’. رومیان را contains the direct object marked by را (‘the Romans’). بخوان is an imperative/hortative form of خواندن used here to mean ‘call/bring/read in’ (command ‘summon’). The order is reportive phrase + temporal marker + object + verb (imperative).
segments.s708-g5: بیامد is a past tense verb ‘came’. نیاطوس is a proper name (the subject). با رومیان is a prepositional phrase ‘with the Romans’ indicating accompaniment. The basic order is Verb (past) + Subject + accompaniment, a common poetic/order‑flexible arrangement in epic Persian.
segments.s708-g6: نشستند is intended as the past plural ‘they sat’ (they took seats); here the form appears but the provided sense list does not include this verbal meaning, so treat it as past action. با فیلسوفان is ‘with the philosophers’ (accompaniment). بخوان follows as an infinitive/imperative-like verb meaning here ‘they sang/recited’ at the feast; the clause therefore says they sat with the philosophers and performed singing/recitation.
segments.s708-g7: چو is a subordinating particle meaning ‘when’ followed by the clause خسرو فرود آمد ‘Khosrow descended’ (چو = when + Subject + past verb آمد). از تخت locates the action ‘from the throne’. بار here is a noun meaning ‘load’/‘baggage’ or more generally ‘the thing borne’ and attaches to تخت to indicate the object taken from the throne area—functionally a part of the noun phrase describing what he came down with.
segments.s708-g8: This is a nominal string describing the garment: ابا جامه روم گوهر نگار = ‘the Roman garment (ابا/آبا) adorned with jewels’. ابا here is the name of a cloak/robe (an Arabic loan in classical Persian), جامه is ‘garment’, روم qualifies it as ‘Roman’, and گوهر نگار is a compound indicating ‘jeweled / gem‑adorned’. Note: two of these senses (ابا and نگار) are not among the provided sense options and are therefore marked as unknown.
segments.s708-g89: توبس is a fused/colloquial/poetic form (تو+بس) meaning “that is enough / cease.” کن is the imperative marker (بس کن = “stop!”). بدین here reads as به + دین (literary order) and introduces the following noun phrase; نیاکان خویش = “your ancestors,” where خویش is the possessive formed from خوی + ش (kinship/root + pronoun). The whole segment functions as an interjectional command: “Enough with the religion/ways of those ancestors of yours.”
segments.s708-g90: خردمند is an adjectival/noun-form meaning “wise (person).” مردم is ‘the people’ and is the syntactic agent/topic here. نگردد is a negative present/past-form of گردیدن (here best read as ‘become / turn into’) with negative prefix ن-, so “does not become.” ز is the poetic preposition meaning “from / because of.” کیش means “sect/creed.” Together: “A wise person does not become so from (or because of) a creed.”
segments.s708-g91: برین گونه = بر + این + گونه, a formula meaning “in this manner / thus.” چون شد literally “as/when it became” (چون used as a conjunction ‘as/when’ + شد ‘became’). سخنها دراز = “the speeches (sakhnhā) [were] long,” with adjective دراز postpositive as is common in Persian. The whole clause is temporal: “When the speeches thus became long…”
segments.s708-g92: به لشکر = the preposition به + لشکر “to/into the army/camp.” گه here is a temporal adverb ‘on that occasion / then.’ آمد is the past tense ‘came.’ نیاطوس is the proper name (subject). باز is the adverb ‘again,’ placed after the name for emphasis: “Niyātus again came to the camp.”
segments.s708-g9: خرامید is a past 3rd-person verb meaning 'he strode/sashayed' (classical narrative past). خندان is an adjective used adverbially ('smiling'), modifying the action. و simply links the two coordinated actions. برخوان is a locative/locutional compound ('at/to the table/feast'), and نشست is a second past verb 'sat (down)'; the two verbs form a sequence: 'he strode ... and sat.'
segments.s708-g10: بشد here functions as a past/eventive verb 'it happened / there came' (classical idiom introducing a new occurrence). نیز is an adverb 'also'. بندوی is the noun subject 'a bondman/servant'. برسم derives from رسید (here 'arrived/reached') describing the servant's coming, and بدست is an adverbial phrase 'in (his) hand' describing how he came or what he bore.
segments.s708-g11: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign/king'. بگرفت is a past-tense verb 'took'/'seized' (simple narrative past). واژ نهان is a nominal phrase: واژ (here used nominally as 'overturn/reversal' or 'upheaval') qualified by نهان 'hidden/secret', i.e. 'a secret overturn/plot'; it functions as the object of بگرفت.
segments.s708-g12: به is the preposition 'to/at'. زمزم refers to the sacred/purifying water or cup (here a place/thing at which a ceremony is made). همی is the classical imperfective/continuative particle (here 'was/then' or 'thereupon') marking the action. رای is 'counsel/plan' (object), and زد is the simple past 'struck/put forth' used idiomatically with رای to mean 'uttered/laid down counsel'. با مهان is the instrumental/associative phrase 'with the guests'.
segments.s708-g13: نیاطوس is a proper name (subject). کان here is a classical particle meaning 'when/that' or 'on that occasion' (not the ore sense), introducing a subordinate temporal/conditional clause. دید is the past 'saw', and بنداخت is a past transitive verb 'threw/cast down' (here taking نان 'bread' as its object).
segments.s708-g14: از آشفتگی is an ablative phrase 'from/out of the confusion' giving cause. باز پس شد is a verbal phrase meaning 'withdrew/stepped back' (باز ... پس together indicate retreat or returning away) with شد as the past auxiliary. ز خوان is a simple prepositional phrase 'from the table/feast' marking the locus he withdrew from.
segments.s708-g15: همی‌گفت is the iterative/past habitual 'he kept saying' (classical progressive). واژ and چلیپا are the content words of his complaint: واژ used as a noun 'overturn/overthrown' and چلیپا 'the cross'; بهم 'together/into disorder' modifies them or indicates they are together. The phrase reports the repeated utterance rather than a full declarative sentence.
segments.s708-g16: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'/'by'. قیصر is 'Caesar' (agent). بود is the past copula 'was/it was', here introducing the claim. بر is the preposition 'against' (indicating hostility directed at), مسیحا is 'the Messiah' (object of the hostility), and ستم is the noun 'oppression/injustice' — together 'that the oppression against the Messiah was by Caesar.'
segments.s708-g81: 'چو' here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. The clause contains two actions: 'مریم برفت' ('Maryam left') followed by an elided subject in the second clause ('(she) said') expressed by 'این سخنها بگفت' — Persian commonly omits the pronoun when it is clear from context. 'برفت' is an old/poetic past form 'went away' and 'بگفت' is simple past 'said'.
segments.s708-g82: Subject 'نیاطوس' governs both verbs: 'بشنید' (past 'heard') and 'کینه نهفت' (literally 'hid hatred' — i.e., 'harbored a grudge'). The coordinating 'و' links the two verbs; 'نهفت' is the past of نهفتن used here to indicate inward concealment of hostility.
segments.s708-g83: 'هم' = 'also' / 'too'. 'از کار بندوی' = 'because of / on account of the bondwoman's deed' (Persian uses the preposition 'از' + noun-phrase to indicate cause). 'دل کرد نرم' literally 'made (his) heart soft' — here 'کرد' with 'دل' expresses that his heart became softened/tendered (a common idiom: دل کردن = to be moved), and 'نرم' is the resulting adjective 'soft/tender'. The subject (he) is omitted but understood from context.
segments.s708-g84: 'کجا داشت' is a rhetorical question 'where/why would he have...' with داشت (past of داشتن) meaning 'have' or 'could have' in this sense. 'از روی بندوی' literally 'because of / from the face of the bondmaid' (روی = face; here the prepositional phrase gives the cause/setting), and 'شرم' is the noun 'shame/modesty'. Word order is flexible: question-word + verb + prepositional phrase + noun.
segments.s708-g85: بیامد is the simple past, 3rd-person singular of آمدن ('came'); the phrase به نزدیک + noun functions adverbially ('near/close to'); خسرو is the name (direct object/target of approach). چو here is the subordinating conjunction 'when/as', and گرد is a short poetic adverbial/particle meaning roughly 'around/near' or 'as he came about' that links into the following clause. Word order is subject/verb then prepositional locality, with the temporal/subordinating چو+گرد introducing the next action.
segments.s708-g86: دل خویش = 'his own heart' (دل 'heart' + خویش represented here as خوی + enclitic ش; ش is the 3rd‑person possessive). خوش کرد is a composed predicate: adjective خوش ('happy/pleasant') with کرد (past 'made/did'), so 'made (it) happy'. زان = 'from that/because of that', and گفته مرد is a possessive/genitive idea 'the man's spoken words' (گفته 'saying/statement' + مرد 'man'); in Persian poetry the ezāfe is often omitted, but the relation is 'گفته‌ی مرد'.
segments.s708-g87: نیاطوس گفت is a reporting clause: proper name نیاطوس (subject) + گفت (past 3rd‑person 'said'). ای جهاندیده شاه is a vocative: ای marks direct address, جهاندیده is an adjective 'worldly‑wise/well‑traveled' placed before شاه 'king'.
segments.s708-g88: خردمندی is the noun object 'wisdom/prudence'. از introduces the source ('from/of'); مست رومی is a noun phrase 'the drunken Roman' (مست 'drunk' qualifying رومی 'Roman/Byzantine'). مخواه is the negative imperative/prohibitive form 'do not seek/ask' (2nd‑person negative), placed at the end in typical Persian SOV order.

sentences.s708-s25: Maryam said to Khosrow thus: 'I will raise a war against this assembly.'
sentences.s708-s26: Give me ten proud bondmen, so that the Romans may —
sentences.s708-s27: so that the Romans may see it, and so that the body may again have its proper rest; no one ever sought war in vain.
sentences.s708-s28: The king sent the bondman to Niatous with ten horsemen.
sentences.s708-s37: Do not let Caesar's suffering and deeds be wasted; remain so that we may be merry for a little while.
sentences.s708-s38: Do it, father — my heart is weary; I have girded a mourning-belt about my waist.
sentences.s708-s39: His heart is entirely full of hatred; his tongue is full of suffering, and his care (too) is so.
sentences.s708-s40: For he, after being overturned, became an ugly-speaker; do not seek wisdom from the foolish.
sentences.s708-s13: Niyātus rose from that spot; he went to his camp and became half-drunk.
sentences.s708-s14: The Roman put on his battle-armor, to ruin the feast.
sentences.s708-s15: All the Roman horsemen, eager for battle, turned their faces toward Khosrow’s court.
sentences.s708-s16: Then the Romans sent cavalry from the host, like the wind, to Khosrow.
sentences.s708-s33: You break the bond of kinship; that very glory of Caesar you will strip from me.
sentences.s708-s34: Have you heard from Caesar that Khosrow will turn from the faith when he comes to the land of Iran?
sentences.s708-s35: Do not utter any unpleasant speech; you, take the bondman and lay his head to your bosom.
sentences.s708-s36: Do you not know that the dehqān does not depart from the ancient faith? Why then do you speak naive words?
sentences.s708-s21: From Kayumarth and Jamshid up to Kay Qobad, no one remembered the Messiah.
sentences.s708-s22: Lest it be that the religion of his ancestors (should) choose his proud elite and his pure ones.
sentences.s708-s23: May I be granted to become of this Messiah; I will not be taken and called 'overturned' and become a pious one.
sentences.s708-s24: You alone always count up crookedness; I have seen skill among the Romans in times of action.
sentences.s708-s17: Who is that wretched servant who would slap the back of his hand against the face of a God‑worshipping man?
sentences.s708-s18: If you send him to my presence, do so; otherwise, see the tumult of the assembly.
sentences.s708-s19: Now turn away from me farther, for he is a wayfarer who continually seeks the imperial throne.
sentences.s708-s20: When Khosrow heard (this) he became angry and said, 'Let no one hide the religion of God.'
sentences.s708-s9: When the bondman saw that, (that one) struck the back of the hand; "Read aloud to the face of the cross-worshipper!"
sentences.s708-s10: When the king saw that deed, grief arose; his countenance was ruined and he withered like the fenugreek flower.
sentences.s708-s11: He said to Gostaham: 'Say this, O fool — one must not act without judgment.'
sentences.s708-s12: What business has he with Niatous the Roman? He has made his own body despised today.
sentences.s708-s29: That same Mary, the clever woman, who was always — her lips kept (silent).
sentences.s708-s30: She said to him, 'Go to the father's brother; say, “O ill‑willed, quarrelsome one!”'
sentences.s708-s31: Did you not see what he said to King Caesar — that, for the sake of greatness, he was his match?
sentences.s708-s32: By ties of kinship and by desire (or favour), he was adorned — by men and by treasure.
sentences.s708-s1: On the next day Khosrow made himself ready and placed that Kayanian crown on his head.
sentences.s708-s2: They set a banquet in the garden. Then he said, 'Call the Romans.'
sentences.s708-s3: Niatōs came with the Romans; they sat down and, together with the philosophers, sang/recited.
sentences.s708-s4: When Khosrow descended from the throne, he took up the Roman jewel‑adorned cloak/robe.
sentences.s708-s45: Enough about those ancestors of yours; a wise person does not become so because of a (mere) creed.
sentences.s708-s46: When the speeches grew long in this manner, Niyātus again came to the army.
sentences.s708-s5: He strode in smiling and sat down at the table; then also a bondman arrived, with (it) in his hand.
sentences.s708-s6: The sovereign seized the hidden overturn (a secret matter), and at the Zamzam he cast his counsel with the guests.
sentences.s708-s7: When Niyātus saw that, he cast down the bread; because of the confusion he withdrew again from the table.
sentences.s708-s8: He kept saying, 'Overturned! and the cross together' — that is, he complained that the Caesar had afflicted the Messiah.
sentences.s708-s41: When Maryam left, she spoke these words; Niyatus heard them and harbored a grudge.
sentences.s708-s42: Also, because of the bondwoman's action his heart grew soft; where could he feel shame before the bondmaid's face?
sentences.s708-s43: He came close to Khosrow; when the man's words had made his heart glad.
sentences.s708-s44: Niyātus said, 'O worldly-wise king, do not seek wisdom from a drunken Roman.'
